cards.side1: Why is ethanoic anhydride used instead of ethanoyl chloride in the preparation of aspirin?
cards.side2: Ethanoic anhydride is cheaper, less corrosive, and does not produce toxic hydrogen chloride gas when it reacts.

cards.side1: Describe the main steps of recrystallization used to purify the crude aspirin.
cards.side2: 1. Dissolve the crude solid in the minimum volume of hot solvent. 2. Perform a hot filtration to remove insoluble impurities. 3. Cool the filtrate in an ice bath to crystallize the aspirin. 4. Filter under reduced pressure.

cards.side1: How do you calculate the atom economy of a reaction?
cards.side2: Atom Economy = (Molar mass of desired product / Sum of molar masses of all reactants) × 100

cards.side1: How can melting point determination indicate the purity of the synthesized aspirin?
cards.side2: Pure aspirin will melt sharply at exactly its theoretical melting point (136°C). Impurities will lower the melting point and cause it to melt over a broader range of temperatures.

cards.side1: What is the main safety hazard of ethanoic anhydride?
cards.side2: It is corrosive and reacts violently with water. Wear gloves and eye protection.
